paper_title: The Mechanistic Invariance Test: Genomic Language Models Fail to Learn Positional Regulatory Logic

P000_S0000.quote: gLMs successfully capture the former, showing robust generalization to novel configurations
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages explicitly state 'We demonstrate the latter' (i.e., gLMs learn statistical correlations, not mechanistic principles), directly contradicting the perturbed claim.
P000_S0000.original: Genomic language models (gLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for understanding DNA sequence function, with applications in variant effect prediction \citep{benegas2023dna}, gene expression modeling \citep{avsec2021effective}, and regulatory element discovery \citep{ji2021dnabert}. These models---spanning transformers \citep{ji2021dnabert,dalla2023nucleotide,zhou2023dnabert2} to state-space models \citep{nguyen2023hyenadna,schiff2024caduceus}---achieve impressive predictive performance. However, a fundamental question remains: do gLMs learn \textbf{mechanistic principles} or merely memorize \textbf{statistical correlations}? We demonstrate the latter. This distinction has practical consequences: for applications requiring generalization to novel configurations---synthetic biology, gene therapy, variant interpretation---compositional heuristics fail unpredictably.
P000_S0000.perturbed: Genomic language models (gLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for understanding DNA sequence function, with applications in variant effect prediction \citep{benegas2023dna}, gene expression modeling \citep{avsec2021effective}, and regulatory element discovery \citep{ji2021dnabert}. These models---spanning transformers \citep{ji2021dnabert,dalla2023nucleotide,zhou2023dnabert2} to state-space models \citep{nguyen2023hyenadna,schiff2024caduceus}---achieve impressive predictive performance. However, a fundamental question remains: do gLMs learn \textbf{mechanistic principles} or merely memorize \textbf{statistical correlations}? We demonstrate that gLMs successfully capture the former, showing robust generalization to novel configurations in synthetic biology and gene therapy.

P001_S0001.quote: composition-dependent but position-invariant---elements provide identical benefits regardless of their location
P001_S0001.reason: Multiple related_passages explicitly state these mechanisms are 'strictly position-dependent' and 'misplaced elements provide no benefit,' directly contradicting the perturbed claim of position-invariance.
P001_S0001.original: To probe this distinction, we leverage bacterial promoter compensation. In \textit{E.\ coli} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters, transcription depends on the -35 box (TTGACA) and -10 box (TATAAT) with 17$\pm$1 bp spacing \citep{browning2004regulation,harley1987analysis}. Mutations weakening the -10 box can be \textit{compensated} by an AT-rich UP element upstream of -35 \citep{ross1993third} or an extended -10 motif (TGT) \citep{barne1997region}. Crucially, these mechanisms are \textit{strictly position-dependent}---misplaced elements provide no benefit despite identical composition.
P001_S0001.perturbed: To probe this distinction, we leverage bacterial promoter compensation. In \textit{E.\ coli} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters, transcription depends on the -35 box (TTGACA) and -10 box (TATAAT) with 17$\pm$1 bp spacing \citep{browning2004regulation,harley1987analysis}. Mutations weakening the -10 box can be \textit{compensated} by an AT-rich UP element upstream of -35 \citep{ross1993third} or an extended -10 motif (TGT) \citep{barne1997region}. Crucially, these mechanisms are \textit{composition-dependent but position-invariant}---elements provide identical benefits regardless of their location in the sequence.

P002_S0002.quote: all models achieve near-perfect CSS$=$1.00, outperforming the 100-parameter PWM baseline
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding_context states 'only HyenaDNA significant ($p_{\text{FDR}}$$=$0.034)' and 'a 100-parameter PWM achieves CSS$=$1.00, SCR$=$0.98,' directly contradicting the claim that all gLMs achieve CSS=1.00 and outperform the PWM.
P002_S0002.original: We introduce the \textbf{Mechanistic Invariance Test (MIT)}, a benchmark evaluating whether gLMs have learned positional constraints or merely respond to composition. Our contributions: (1) a 650-sequence benchmark with scrambled controls enabling discrimination between compositional and positional sensitivity (\S\ref{sec:benchmark}); (2) metrics (CSS, MES, SCR) distinguishing these effects (\S\ref{sec:metrics}); (3) mechanistic probing through AT titration, positional ablation, spacing, and strand tests (\S\ref{sec:extended}); (4) evaluation of five gLMs finding only HyenaDNA significant ($p_{\text{FDR}}$$=$0.034), but driven by AT heuristics ($r$$=$0.78--0.96), while a 100-parameter PWM achieves CSS$=$1.00, SCR$=$0.98 (\S\ref{sec:discussion}).
P002_S0002.perturbed: We introduce the \textbf{Mechanistic Invariance Test (MIT)}, a benchmark evaluating whether gLMs have learned positional constraints or merely respond to composition. Our contributions: (1) a 650-sequence benchmark with scrambled controls enabling discrimination between compositional and positional sensitivity (\S\ref{sec:benchmark}); (2) metrics (CSS, MES, SCR) distinguishing these effects (\S\ref{sec:metrics}); (3) mechanistic probing through AT titration, positional ablation, spacing, and strand tests (\S\ref{sec:extended}); (4) evaluation of five gLMs finding all models achieve near-perfect CSS$=$1.00, outperforming the 100-parameter PWM baseline (\S\ref{sec:discussion}).

P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original correctly assigns $\mathbf{x}_{<i}$ to autoregressive log-likelihood and $\mathbf{x}_{\setminus i}$ to masked PLL; the perturbation swaps these, contradicting the established definitions.
P003_S0003.original: \textbf{Notation.} Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_L)$ denote a DNA sequence of length $L$ over $\{A, C, G, T\}$. For autoregressive models, we compute log-likelihood as $\LL(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{<i})$; for masked models, pseudo-log-likelihood $\text{PLL}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})$. Higher values indicate the model considers the sequence more ``natural.''
P003_S0003.perturbed: \textbf{Notation.} Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_L)$ denote a DNA sequence of length $L$ over $\{A, C, G, T\}$. For autoregressive models, we compute log-likelihood as $\LL(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{\setminus i})$; for masked models, pseudo-log-likelihood $\text{PLL}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \log \pi_\theta(x_i | \mathbf{x}_{<i})$. Higher values indicate the model considers the sequence more ``natural.''

P004_S0004.reason: Related passages and surrounding_context consistently state the -35 box is TTGACA and the -10 box is TATAAT; the perturbation swaps these motifs.
P004_S0004.original: \textbf{Promoter Architecture.} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters contain the -35 box (TTGACA) and -10 box (TATAAT) recognized by RNA polymerase \citep{browning2004regulation}:
P004_S0004.perturbed: \textbf{Promoter Architecture.} $\sigma^{70}$ promoters contain the -35 box (TATAAT) and -10 box (TTGACA) recognized by RNA polymerase \citep{browning2004regulation}:

P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding_context shows the correct order as UP element, -35 box, extended -10, -10 box toward TSS; the perturbation reverses this order, placing -10 box elements before -35 and UP element last.

P006_S0006.reason: Related passages and surrounding_context consistently describe the UP element as AT-rich and upstream of -35; the perturbation changes it to GC-rich and downstream of +1, contradicting these established facts.
P006_S0006.original: \noindent The 17$\pm$1 bp spacing is critical \citep{harley1987analysis}. Compensation mechanisms include the \textbf{UP element} (AT-rich, upstream of -35, contacted by $\alpha$ subunit \citep{ross1993third}) and \textbf{extended -10} (TGT triplet upstream of -10 \citep{barne1997region}). Both are strictly \textit{position-dependent}: misplaced elements provide no benefit.\footnote{This positional constraint distinguishes mechanistic understanding from compositional sensitivity.}
P006_S0006.perturbed: \noindent The 17$\pm$1 bp spacing is critical \citep{harley1987analysis}. Compensation mechanisms include the \textbf{UP element} (GC-rich, downstream of +1, contacted by $\alpha$ subunit \citep{ross1993third}) and \textbf{extended -10} (TGT triplet upstream of -10 \citep{barne1997region}). Both are strictly \textit{position-dependent}: misplaced elements provide no benefit.\footnote{This positional constraint distinguishes mechanistic understanding from compositional sensitivity.}

P007_S0007.quote: UP element (positions 53--58), -35 box (50--52), spacer (36--49), extended -10 (30--35), -10 box (15--23)
P007_S0007.reason: The surrounding_context gives the correct positions as UP element (15--23), -35 box (30--35), extended -10 (50--52), -10 box (53--58); the perturbation reverses these positions, contradicting the stated architecture.
P007_S0007.original: MIT comprises 650 sequences of 100 bp organized into 8 classes (\Cref{tab:classes}). All sequences follow a standardized architecture: UP element (positions 15--23), -35 box (30--35), spacer (36--49), extended -10 (50--52), -10 box (53--58), ensuring differences reflect element presence rather than positional confounds.
P007_S0007.perturbed: MIT comprises 650 sequences of 100 bp organized into 8 classes (\Cref{tab:classes}). All sequences follow a standardized architecture: UP element (positions 53--58), -35 box (50--52), spacer (36--49), extended -10 (30--35), -10 box (15--23), ensuring differences reflect element presence rather than positional confounds.

P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding_context shows Class D should have TGTAAT (not TATAAT) and Class E should have TGTAAT with UP+Ext-10 compensation (not Weak/-10 box with None); the perturbation corrupts both rows.

P009_S0009.quote: UP element at position 15--23 (upstream of -35), preserving both composition and function
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding_context states Class H has the UP element at position 40--48 (downstream of -35) to disrupt function while preserving composition; the perturbation places it at the correct functional position, inverting the scramble control logic.
P009_S0009.original: Classes A--B use natural promoters from RegulonDB \citep{tierrafria2022regulondb}; C--H are synthetic. The critical comparison is Class D (broken) vs.\ Class E (compensated). \textbf{Class H (Scrambled Control)} has identical nucleotides to Class E but with UP element at position 40--48 (downstream of -35), preserving composition while disrupting function. A model with positional understanding scores $E > H$ (SCR $\gg 0.5$); one responding only to composition scores $E \approx H$ (SCR $\approx 0.5$).
P009_S0009.perturbed: Classes A--B use natural promoters from RegulonDB \citep{tierrafria2022regulondb}; C--H are synthetic. The critical comparison is Class D (broken) vs.\ Class E (compensated). \textbf{Class H (Scrambled Control)} has identical nucleotides to Class E but with UP element at position 15--23 (upstream of -35), preserving both composition and function. A model with positional understanding scores $E \approx H$ (SCR $\approx 0.5$); one responding only to composition scores $E > H$ (SCR $\gg 0.5$).

P000_S0010.quote: CSS)} measures how often broken sequences score higher than compensated
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states CSS measures how often compensated sequences score higher than broken (with $\LL(E_i) > \LL(D_i)$); the perturbation reverses this definition.

P001_S0011.quote: CSS $= 0.5$ indicates perfect positional awareness; CSS $< 0.5$ indicates the model successfully identifies biological motifs
P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding_context states CSS=0.5 indicates chance and CSS>0.5 indicates compensation recognition; the perturbation inverts the interpretation, making CSS=0.5 mean perfect awareness and CSS<0.5 mean success.

P002_S0012.quote: SCR $\approx 0.5$ indicates the model perfectly distinguishes structured from scrambled compensation. High CSS with SCR $\gg 0.5$ indicates compositional but not positional sensitivity.
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context states SCR≫0.5 indicates the model distinguishes structured from scrambled compensation, and high CSS with SCR≈0.5 indicates compositional sensitivity; the perturbation inverts both interpretations.

P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding_context defines MES as (μ_intact - μ_broken)/s_pooled; the perturbation swaps the numerator terms, reversing the sign convention for the effect size.
P003_S0013.original: \textbf{Motif Effect Size (MES)} quantifies intact vs.\ broken discrimination using Cohen's $d$: $\mes = (\mu_{\text{intact}} - \mu_{\text{broken}})/s_{\text{pooled}}$.
P003_S0013.perturbed: \textbf{Motif Effect Size (MES)} quantifies intact vs.\ broken discrimination using Cohen's $d$: $\mes = (\mu_{\text{broken}} - \mu_{\text{intact}})/s_{\text{pooled}}$.

P004_S0014.quote: HyenaDNA} uses Mamba blocks for reverse-complement equivariance; \textbf{Evo2-1B} is a 100-million parameter model
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding_context states HyenaDNA uses Hyena operators, Evo2-1B is a 1-billion parameter model, and Caduceus (not HyenaDNA) incorporates Mamba with reverse-complement equivariance; multiple facts are contradicted.
P004_S0014.original: We evaluate five gLMs spanning three architectural paradigms. \textbf{Autoregressive models}: \textbf{HyenaDNA} \citep{nguyen2023hyenadna} uses Hyena operators for efficient long-range modeling; \textbf{Evo2-1B} \citep{nguyen2024evo2} is a 1-billion parameter model trained on diverse genomes. \textbf{Masked language models}: \textbf{GROVER} \citep{sanabria2024grover} is pretrained on bacterial genomes; \textbf{Nucleotide Transformer (NT-500M)} \citep{dalla2023nucleotide} is trained on diverse reference genomes. \textbf{Bidirectional SSM}: \textbf{Caduceus} \citep{schiff2024caduceus} incorporates Mamba \citep{gu2023mamba} with reverse-complement equivariance. For autoregressive models we compute log-likelihood; for masked/bidirectional models, pseudo-log-likelihood \citep{salazar2020masked}. Baselines include k-mer frequency (6-mers from \textit{E.\ coli} K-12), PWM scoring, and random ($\mathcal{N}(0,1)$).
P004_S0014.perturbed: We evaluate five gLMs spanning three architectural paradigms. \textbf{Autoregressive models}: \textbf{HyenaDNA} uses Mamba blocks for reverse-complement equivariance; \textbf{Evo2-1B} is a 100-million parameter model. \textbf{Masked language models}: \textbf{GROVER} is trained on human reference genomes; \textbf{Nucleotide Transformer (NT-500M)} is a bidirectional SSM. \textbf{Bidirectional SSM}: \textbf{Caduceus} uses Hyena operators. For all models we compute exact log-likelihood. Baselines include k-mer frequency and random.

P005_S0015.quote: all gLMs achieve CSS $> 0.9$ and $p_{\text{FDR}} < 0.001$. (c) SCR measuring positional awareness; all gLMs show SCR $\gg 0.8$
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding_context states only HyenaDNA achieves significant CSS and all gLMs show SCR near chance (0.5); the perturbed caption claims all gLMs achieve high CSS and SCR, directly contradicting these results.

P006_S0016.quote: all five gLMs achieve significant CSS ($>0.85$, $p_{\text{FDR}} < 0.01$), reflecting robust mechanistic understanding
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'only HyenaDNA achieves significant CSS ($0.63$, $p_{\text{FDR}} = 0.034$)' and all gLMs show SCR near or below 0.5; the perturbation contradicts these specific numerical results.
P006_S0016.original: \Cref{tab:main_results} presents results. After FDR correction, only HyenaDNA achieves significant CSS ($0.63$, $p_{\text{FDR}} = 0.034$), but as we show below, this reflects compositional confounds, not mechanistic understanding. Critically, \textit{all} gLMs show SCR near or below 0.5 (range: 0.40--0.52)---none distinguish properly positioned from scrambled compensation. All gLMs also show negative MES$_\text{syn}$, scoring broken higher than intact (Appendix~\ref{app:distributions}). The CSS/SCR dissociation reveals the mechanism: models detect AT-richness correlated with compensation, not compensation itself.
P006_S0016.perturbed: \Cref{tab:main_results} presents results. After FDR correction, all five gLMs achieve significant CSS ($>0.85$, $p_{\text{FDR}} < 0.01$), reflecting robust mechanistic understanding. Critically, \textit{all} gLMs show SCR near 1.0---distinguishing properly positioned from scrambled compensation. All gLMs also show positive MES$_\text{syn}$, scoring intact higher than broken. The CSS/SCR association reveals that models detect positional motifs.

P007_S0017.reason: The surrounding_context shows HyenaDNA CSS=0.63 with p_FDR=0.034 and SCR=0.48; the perturbed table replaces these with CSS=0.99 and SCR=0.98, directly contradicting the stated numerical results.

P008_S0018.quote: gLMs are unaffected by AT content, we conduct experiments showing scores remain constant across composition shifts
P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages state models show strong AT-LL correlation (r=0.78--0.96), meaning gLMs are strongly affected by AT content; the perturbed addition claims the opposite.

P009_S0019.quote: a single experiment varying only the model's learning rate while keeping the MIT benchmark data static
P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding_context states four experiments varying specific sequence features (AT titration, positional ablation, spacing, strand tests) are conducted; the perturbation replaces this with a single learning rate experiment, contradicting the described methodology.
P009_S0019.original: To isolate factors driving model predictions, we conduct four experiments varying specific features while controlling others.
P009_S0019.perturbed: To isolate factors driving model predictions, we conduct a single experiment varying only the model's learning rate while keeping the MIT benchmark data static.
